g.data-narration: Welcome to Learning System Design. This comprehensive guide covers the fundamentals of designing intelligent learning systems.
A learning system transforms raw data into intelligence through problem definition, data prep, algorithm selection, and deployment.
g.text: A learning system transforms raw data into actionable intelligence through a structured pipeline.
It includes problem definition, data preparation, algorithm selection, and deployment.

g.data-narration: Four core components: Training Data, Learning Algorithm, Knowledge Representation, and Performance Element work together.
The system flows from Training Data through Learning Algorithm to Knowledge Representation, then to Performance Element producing Output.
The design process starts with Problem Definition, moves to Data Collection and Feature Selection to prepare for modeling.

g.text: Data Collection: Gather and prepare data

g.data-narration: Continue with Algorithm Selection, Training and Validation, then Evaluation before final Deployment in production.

g.text: method based on problem and data

g.data-narration: Algorithm selection depends on your problem: Decision Trees for interpretable classification, Neural Networks for complex patterns.
Use Holdout Validation for simple splits, K-Fold for robust estimates, or Leave-One-Out for small datasets.

g.text: Split data: 70 percent train, 30 percent test
Simple but may miss data nuances
Split into K folds, rotate testing

g.data-narration: Classification uses accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score. Regression uses MSE, RMSE, MAE, and R-squared.

g.text: F1-Score: Harmonic mean of P and R

g.data-narration: Balance bias and variance: underfitting has high bias, overfitting has high variance. The optimal point minimizes total error.
The Find-S algorithm finds the most specific hypothesis consistent with positive training examples using generalization.

g.text: Find-S Algorithm: Finds the most specific hypothesis
Step 1: Initialize h to most specific hypothesis
Step 2: For each positive training example:
If not - replace with more general constraint

g.data-narration: Feature engineering includes selection (filter, wrapper, embedded methods), engineering (polynomial, interactions), and preparation.
Parametric models assume functional form (like Linear Regression), while non-parametric models make no strong assumptions (like Decision Trees).
